nativeRange.habitat: Clear coastal coral reefs in bays and semi-exposed reef edges, generally 3-20 m depth and most often 3-8 m, among living stony corals.
nativeRange.biotope: Live coral cover on shallow tropical reefs.

decorPreferences: established reef with multiple live stony coral colonies
ample live rock with crevices for perching and shelter

diet.notes: Obligate corallivore that feeds primarily on the superficial coenosarc tissue of live stony coral polyps. Some individuals have been reported to accept frozen mysis, brine shrimp, chopped crustacean flesh, and herbivore preparations, but most specimens require a steady supply of sacrificial stony coral colonies to survive long-term in captivity.

avoidWith: other combtooth blennies of similar form
any reefkeeper unwilling to sacrifice live stony corals
aggressive fish that would harass it off its perches

summary: An Indo-Pacific combtooth blenny with a leopard-spotted body and feathery cirri above the eyes. Generally considered an expert-only fish because it is an obligate corallivore that grazes the living tissue of stony corals.
careNotes: The leopard blenny is one of the few aquarium-traded fish that is a true obligate corallivore — it feeds on the superficial coenosarc tissue of live SPS and other stony corals and is generally considered incompatible with display reef tanks where coral preservation matters. Best reserved for advanced keepers willing to maintain a dedicated tank with a rotating supply of sacrificial coral colonies. A few specimens have been reported to transition onto frozen and prepared foods, but starvation is the most common outcome when offered only standard aquarium fare. Tank-size guidance varies widely: LiveAquaria lists a 30 US gal (~115 L) minimum aimed at the fish's small body size, while practical husbandry guides emphasize that an obligate coral-eater needs a mature reef system with enough live coral biomass to sustain it — biased upward to 300 L to reflect that constraint. Like other combtooth blennies, has been known to jump, so a tightly fitted lid is generally advised. Generally peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but tends to be aggressive toward other similar-bodied blennies. Family Blenniidae, order Blenniiformes per FishBase and Wikipedia; the genus Exallias is monotypic. Reef Compatible: with caution is a generous reading — for SPS/LPS reefkeepers this fish is effectively not reef-safe.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing. Eggs are demersal and attached to the substrate via an adhesive pad; males prepare nesting sites by overgrazing coral patches. Captive breeding is not routinely accomplished in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference: size 5.5 in, temp 72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, min 30 gal, peaceful, reef compatibility 'with caution', care level Expert Only, obligate corallivore.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy and wild biology: Family Blenniidae, Order Blenniiformes, max 14.5 cm TL, Indo-Pacific (Red Sea to Hawaii and Society Islands, north to Ryukyu/Bonin, south to New Caledonia/Rapa), depth 3-20 m, preferred temperature 24.7-29.3 C (mean 28.3 C), corallivorous, oviparous with distinct pairing.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-check on classification and diet: monotypic genus Exallias, max length 14.5 cm TL, Indo-Pacific incl. Hawaii, Red Sea, Australia and French Polynesia, feeds on superficial coenosarc tissue of coral polyps and has been observed to cause localized coral bleaching.

fish.breedingNotes: Demersal egg layers with male nest preparation; not routinely reared in home aquariums.
